dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to EMS for a sick person at an apartment. Forty-two-year-old female with altered mental status.
Hi ma'am, I'm with EMS. We're here to check on you and help you tonight.
She has not been acting right. She's been drowsy and not herself.
Can you tell me what is bothering you right now?
I have a headache and I feel sick.
She's been drowsy, nauseous, vomiting, and just not acting right.
She opens her eyes to voice but is drowsy and keeps drifting off.
She answers slowly and has to be asked questions multiple times, but her speech is clear.
She follows commands and moves all extremities appropriately.
Her Glasgow Coma Scale is 13 with confusion and somnolence.
Have you had nausea, vomiting, headache, or any other symptoms tonight?
I've had a headache and I felt nauseous.
Did anything happen before you started feeling this way?
No, we don't know of anything she did before this change in behavior.
Have you used any drugs or alcohol tonight?
Not in front of them. I need to tell you something once we're in the ambulance.
Do you have any allergies or medical problems we should know about?
I'm allergic to penicillin. I have high blood pressure, chronic pain, migraines, and swelling problems.
Airway is patent and self-maintained.
Breathing is present and adequate with clear equal lung sounds bilaterally. No respiratory distress noted.
Strong pulses, good sensation, and movement are present in all extremities.
Skin is warm and dry.
Pupils are constricted, pinpoint, and sluggish.
Blood pressure is 150 over 84.
Pulse is 77.
Respiratory rate is 14.
Four-lead cardiac monitoring is established and continuous monitoring is started.
Head and face are atraumatic and symmetric. Speech is clear. Mucous membranes are moist and pink. Neck is supple without jugular venous distention or pain.
Chest rises symmetrically, and she denies chest pain or trouble breathing.
Abdomen is soft and non-tender with no abdominal pain.
No edema is noted. She reports chronic baseline pain in her back and right knee. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.
This presentation is consistent with altered mental status with nausea and vomiting, with concern for opioid and alcohol ingestion. We are continuing those protocols.
Patient is assisted to the stretcher and secured with five safety straps for transport.
A 20-gauge IV is established in the left forearm and normal saline is started at a keep-vein-open rate.
Ondansetron 4 milligrams IV is administered for nausea.
Patient is given an emesis bag and a blanket for comfort.
Now that we're away from my family, I need to tell you I bought what I thought was a Percocet 10 off the street and took one pill. I also took two Advil PM and drank wine coolers.
Nausea improves after Zofran, and there is no further emesis.
She remains drowsy and confused but is calm, cooperative, and can be redirected to keep her eyes open.
Respirations and oxygen saturation remain adequate without difficulty, and Narcan is not required at this time.
I did not want to go at first.
Because of your drowsiness, confusion, and inability to answer questions appropriately, you need evaluation in the emergency department. This is implied consent.
I understand. I'll go.
We're transporting you to [COMPANY_NAME] Emergency Department, the closest appropriate facility.
Report is called ahead by phone to the emergency department.
At [COMPANY_NAME], patient is moved to ED room 7 and transferred to the ED stretcher. Report is given to the RN and physician at bedside, signatures are obtained, and care is transferred to staff.
